claims.claim_text: Unsourced August 2023 SaaS war story with 200 microservices, 12 TB/day, i3.2xlarge nodes, $22,000/month, 30% engineer time, and a 4-hour outage during a 7.x->8.x upgrade.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In August 2023, a SaaS company running 200 microservices on EKS generated 12 TB of logs per day. They ran a self-managed Elasticsearch cluster on Kubernetes -- 9 data nodes, 3 master nodes, each on i3.2xlarge instances with local NVMe storage. Total monthly cost: $22,000 for compute alone. The cluster required a dedicated engineer spending roughly 30% of their time on shard rebalancing, index lifecycle management, JVM tuning, and version upgrades. When they attempted an upgrade from Elasticsearch 7.x to 8.x, a mapping incompatibility brought down the cluster for 4 hours. During those 4 hours, the security team could not search logs to investigate an active incident.
claims.suggested_rewrite: At scale, self-managing Elasticsearch or OpenSearch for Kubernetes logging can consume substantial infrastructure and engineering time, and upgrade mistakes can temporarily disrupt incident response when log search is unavailable.
claims.rationale: This is a precise named-entity operational anecdote with no attributable source, so the false specifics need to be removed.

claims.claim_text: Unsourced migration outcome claim: three-week move to Amazon OpenSearch Service, engineer time dropping from 30% to 5%, and zero unplanned outages for 14 months.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: They migrated to Amazon OpenSearch Service (managed). The migration took three weeks. The managed service handles node replacement, automated snapshots, encryption, and version upgrades. The same engineer now spends 5% of their time on search operations. More importantly, the cluster has not had a single unplanned outage in 14 months. The lesson: running a distributed search cluster on Kubernetes is technically possible, but the operational overhead is enormous. Managed search services let you focus on what matters -- getting insights from your data.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many teams move logging workloads to managed search services to reduce routine cluster maintenance, use built-in operational features, and lower the risk of upgrade- or node-related outages.
claims.rationale: The specific migration duration, staffing percentage, and outage-free period are uncited outcomes from the same unsourced anecdote.

claims.claim_text: Kafka buffering prevents data loss when OpenSearch is slow or unavailable.
claims.span_hint: section: Buffering with Kafka for Resilience, intro sentence
claims.anchor_text: For high-volume clusters, buffer logs through Kafka to prevent data loss when OpenSearch is slow or unavailable:
claims.suggested_rewrite: For high-volume clusters, buffer logs through Kafka to absorb downstream slowdowns and reduce the risk of log loss when OpenSearch is slow or unavailable:
claims.rationale: Preventing data loss is too strong as a blanket guarantee because the outcome depends on buffering and retry configuration.

claims.claim_text: A 200-pod cluster can generate 50 GB/day of logs, leading to 1.5 TB after a month.
claims.span_hint: section: Index Lifecycle Management (ILM/ISM), opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: A single day of logs for a 200-pod cluster can be 50 GB. After a month, you have 1.5 TB of indices, most of which nobody searches.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A single day of Kubernetes logs can consume a large amount of storage, and retention multiplies that footprint quickly if you do not age data out.
claims.rationale: These are illustrative volume numbers presented as facts without a source.

claims.claim_text: Document-level security can restrict which documents a user sees, and field-level security can include or exclude fields from search hits.
claims.span_hint: section: Fine-Grained Access Control, paragraph after role example
claims.rationale: AWS documents DLS and FLS directly, including that DLS limits matching documents and FLS controls which fields are visible.

claims.claim_text: A practical shard-size target is roughly 10-50 GB per shard, with AWS guidance differentiating search-heavy and log-analytics workloads.
claims.span_hint: section: Sharding and Replication Strategy, Shard Sizing Rules table row 1
claims.rationale: AWS guidance explicitly gives 10-30 GiB for search-latency-sensitive workloads and 30-50 GiB for write-heavy log analytics.

claims.claim_text: Max 20 shards per GB of JVM heap.
claims.span_hint: section: Sharding and Replication Strategy, Shard Sizing Rules table row 2, guideline cell
claims.anchor_text: Max 20 per GB of JVM heap
claims.suggested_rewrite: Use your platform's current shard-per-node guidance instead of a fixed shards-per-GB rule
claims.rationale: This hard number does not line up with current AWS guidance and should not be taught as a stable universal rule.

claims.claim_text: 1000 shards on a node with 32 GB heap is the practical max.
claims.span_hint: section: Sharding and Replication Strategy, Shard Sizing Rules table row 2, reason cell
claims.anchor_text: 1000 shards on a node with 32 GB heap is the practical max
claims.suggested_rewrite: Validate shard budgets against your service's documented per-node limits rather than a single heap-size example
claims.rationale: The 32 GB example is not a stable cross-version rule and current service limits are documented differently.

claims.claim_text: Clusters should be monitored and alerted above 10,000 total shards.
claims.span_hint: section: Sharding and Replication Strategy, Shard Sizing Rules table row 4, guideline cell
claims.suggested_rewrite: Monitor total shard count and alert before you approach your service's documented per-node shard ceilings
claims.rationale: A single cluster-wide 10,000 threshold is topology-dependent and not a universal platform limit.

claims.claim_text: Filter context avoids relevance scoring and frequently used filters are cached, which is why it is preferable for exact-match operational queries.
claims.span_hint: section: Query Optimization, Query Performance Tips table row 1
claims.rationale: Elastic's query/filter reference states that filters do not compute relevance scores and that frequently used filters are cached, but elastic.co is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Exact-match operational queries should target keyword or other term-level fields rather than text fields, because text is analyzed.
claims.span_hint: section: Query Optimization, Query Performance Tips table row 3
claims.rationale: Elastic's term-query docs explicitly warn against using term queries on text fields because text is analyzed, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Search returns 10 hits by default, and large result windows increase per-shard memory and CPU work.
claims.span_hint: section: Query Optimization, Query Performance Tips table row 4
claims.rationale: Elastic's pagination docs directly state the default of 10 hits and describe the memory/CPU cost of large pages, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: OpenSearch originated as a 2021 fork from Elasticsearch 7.10.2/Kibana 7.10.2 after Elastic changed the licensing for newer releases.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.rationale: AWS's launch post directly states the 7.10.2 lineage and the licensing-triggered fork context.

claims.claim_text: OpenSearch/Amazon OpenSearch Service includes features such as anomaly detection and observability or trace-analytics capabilities.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.rationale: AWS's OpenSearch announcement enumerates anomaly detection and trace analytics among OpenSearch features.

claims.claim_text: A shard is a self-contained Lucene index, so shard count and size directly affect performance and coordination overhead.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 2
claims.rationale: Elastic's index fundamentals page directly states that each shard is a Lucene index and that shard number and size affect performance, but elastic.co is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Force-merge reduces index size by 40-60% and speeds queries.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.anchor_text: **The `force_merge` operation during the warm phase can reduce index size by 40-60%** because it compacts multiple Lucene segments into one. This also speeds up queries because there are fewer segments to search. But force merge is CPU-intensive and should only run on warm/cold indices that are no longer receiving writes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **The `force_merge` operation during the warm phase can reclaim space and reduce segment overhead** because it compacts multiple Lucene segments into fewer larger ones. It can help some read-heavy queries, but it is CPU-intensive and should only run on indices that are no longer receiving writes.
claims.rationale: The precise 40-60% size-reduction figure is unsupported and too specific to leave in place.

claims.claim_text: Document Level Security adds 5-15% overhead per query.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4
claims.anchor_text: **Document Level Security in OpenSearch evaluates a filter query on every search request**, which adds 5-15% overhead per query. For high-traffic dashboards, pre-filter by creating separate index aliases per team with built-in filters, which eliminates the per-query DLS evaluation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Document Level Security in OpenSearch applies an access-control filter during searches**. On busy dashboards it can add measurable overhead, so test alternative tenancy patterns if query latency becomes a bottleneck.
claims.rationale: The 5-15% figure is not backed by an authoritative source in the allowlisted set.

claims.claim_text: Without a buffer, OpenSearch backpressure causes Fluent Bit to drop logs.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, table row 'Not buffering through Kafka for high-volume clusters'
claims.anchor_text: Without a buffer, OpenSearch backpressure causes Fluent Bit to drop logs
claims.suggested_rewrite: Without a durable buffer, downstream backpressure can delay ingestion and may eventually lead to log loss if the collector exhausts its available memory or storage buffers
claims.rationale: Direct log loss is configuration-dependent, so the blanket statement is too strong without a source and conditions.

claims.claim_text: AWS guidance ties sustained high JVM memory pressure to GC pause risk and uses keeping usage below 80% as a rule of thumb.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, table row 'Ignoring JVM heap pressure on managed clusters'
claims.rationale: AWS troubleshooting guidance explicitly connects high JVM memory pressure to GC problems and says to keep usage below 80% as a rule of thumb.

claims.claim_text: Force-merge should be used only on read-only indices because writes and updates continue creating new segments.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, table row 'Running force_merge on hot indices'
claims.rationale: Elastic's force-merge API docs directly recommend force-merging only read-only indices and explain why, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Default dynamic mapping maps strings to `text`.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, table row 'Using `text` type for fields that need exact matching'
claims.anchor_text: Default dynamic mapping maps strings to `text`
claims.suggested_rewrite: Default mappings do not always produce the exact field types you want for operational filters and aggregations
claims.rationale: This overstates default mapping behavior and should be rewritten more generally unless a specific engine/version source is supplied.

further_reading.title: Index State Management in Amazon OpenSearch Service
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary AWS guide for lifecycle policies, rollover, and automated retention.

further_reading.title: Fine-grained access control in Amazon OpenSearch Service
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers document-level security, field-level security, roles, and mappings for shared clusters.

further_reading.title: Choosing the number of shards
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides AWS shard-sizing guidance and sizing tradeoffs directly relevant to logging workloads.

notes: Quiz items, exercises, code blocks, and Mermaid diagrams were intentionally excluded per pipeline rules. Table rows were seeded only where they make load-bearing numeric or platform-behavior claims.
